identity.formula: C171H267N51O53S2 (free base; acetate salt form: C171H267N51O53S2·x C2H4O2, 3 ≤ x ≤ 8)
modifications: Intramolecular disulfide bridge between Cys2 and Cys7
Three proline substitutions vs human amylin: Pro25 (replaces Ala), Pro28 (replaces Ser), Pro29 (replaces Ser)
identity.structureClass: linear cyclic peptide (single intrachain disulfide) amylin analog

tile.mechanismSummary: Synthetic analog of pancreatic amylin that agonizes amylin receptors (heterodimers of the calcitonin receptor with RAMP1/2/3) to slow gastric emptying, suppress postprandial glucagon, and reduce food intake.
tile.localization: Peripheral (gastric emptying, pancreatic alpha-cell suppression) and central (area postrema / brainstem satiety circuits).
clinicalUses: Adjunct to mealtime insulin in adults with type 1 diabetes who have not achieved target glycemic control
Adjunct to mealtime insulin in adults with type 2 diabetes who have not achieved target glycemic control
sideEffects: Severe hypoglycemia (boxed warning, especially in type 1 diabetes when combined with insulin)

dosing.quick: FDA-approved, subcutaneous, dosed before each major meal. In type 1 diabetes: start 15 mcg SC, titrate in 15 mcg steps to 30-60 mcg as tolerated. In type 2 diabetes: start 60 mcg SC, titrate to 120 mcg if tolerated. Reduce mealtime insulin by 50% when initiating.
dosing.adminRoute: subcutaneous injection (abdomen or thigh) before meals; do not mix with insulin in same syringe

receptors: AMY1 receptor (CTR + RAMP1) — preferred pramlintide target

steps: Pramlintide binds AMY1/AMY3 receptors on gastric smooth muscle and vagal afferents
Gastric emptying is slowed
Rate of nutrient delivery to small intestine is reduced
Postprandial plasma glucose rise is blunted
Pramlintide engages amylin receptors in CNS and on pancreatic alpha cells (indirect)
Postprandial glucagon secretion is suppressed
Hepatic glucose output is reduced in the postprandial window
Pramlintide acts at amylin receptors in the area postrema
Satiety signaling to hypothalamic appetite circuits increases
Meal size and caloric intake decrease

expanded.humanEvidence: Supported by multiple randomized controlled trials in adults with type 1 and type 2 diabetes showing modest HbA1c reductions (~0.3-0.5%) and modest weight loss (1-2 kg) vs placebo when added to insulin. FDA-approved on this basis in 2005. Not studied in pediatrics and not approved for obesity as monotherapy.
expanded.animalEvidence: Preclinical rodent and canine work established amylin's role in slowing gastric emptying, suppressing glucagon, and reducing food intake; the Des-Lys1 metabolite is biologically active in rats.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Pramlintide differs from human amylin by three proline substitutions (Ala25Pro, Ser28Pro, Ser29Pro) chosen to reduce the amyloidogenicity that plagues human amylin. Functionally it is a non-selective agonist of amylin receptors (heterodimers of the class B calcitonin GPCR with RAMP1/2/3 = AMY1/AMY2/AMY3) with slight preference for AMY1; it also weakly activates the bare calcitonin receptor. Downstream signaling is primarily Gs → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP. Physiologically: (1) slowing of gastric emptying delays carbohydrate delivery to the small intestine; (2) suppression of inappropriately elevated postprandial glucagon reduces hepatic glucose output; (3) activation of area postrema amylin receptors enhances satiety and reduces caloric intake. Plasma half-life is ~48 minutes, requiring dosing before each major meal. Primary metabolite (Des-Lys1 pramlintide) retains activity. Cleared mainly via the kidneys.
expanded.safetyDetail: Boxed warning for severe insulin-induced hypoglycemia, particularly in type 1 diabetes; mealtime insulin must be reduced by 50% when starting pramlintide. Contraindicated in patients with confirmed gastroparesis or hypoglycemia unawareness. Gastrointestinal side effects (nausea, vomiting, anorexia) are dose-limiting and titration-dependent. No increased risk of cardiovascular events has been established, but cardiovascular outcomes trials have not been conducted. Not recommended in pediatrics. Pharmacokinetics in end-stage renal disease and hepatic impairment are not established.

safetyRisks.label: Severe hypoglycemia
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Adjunct therapy can reduce postprandial glucose spikes and support insulin-dose reduction
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Combining pramlintide with mealtime insulin can precipitate severe, potentially disabling hypoglycemia if insulin is not appropriately reduced

safetyRisks.label: Nausea / vomiting / gastroparesis-like symptoms

safetyRisks.harmSummary: Nausea and vomiting are common, dose-limiting, and pramlintide is contraindicated in patients with confirmed gastroparesis

enhancementPotential.summary: No evidence base for metabolic or cosmetic 'enhancement' in healthy-weight, non-diabetic adults. Weight effects in diabetic populations are small (1-2 kg).
enhancementPotential.caveat: Off-label use for weight loss in non-diabetic, non-obese individuals is not supported by trials, carries hypoglycemia risk when combined with any insulin secretagogue, and is not reflected in the FDA label.

missingEvidence: Data in end-stage renal disease / dialysis
Head-to-head trials vs GLP-1 receptor agonists for weight loss in non-diabetic populations
anecdotalUse: Occasionally used off-label for appetite suppression and weight loss in obesity, largely supplanted by cagrilintide and GLP-1 combinations

identity.structureClass: oxyntomodulin-based lipidated peptide (GLP-1R / GCGR dual agonist)

classification.mechanismFamily: GLP-1 receptor / glucagon receptor dual agonist

tile.mechanismSummary: Oxyntomodulin-based dual agonist of the GLP-1 receptor and glucagon receptor; GLP-1R activation drives appetite suppression, delayed gastric emptying, and glucose-dependent insulin secretion, while glucagon receptor activation is thought to increase energy expenditure and hepatic fat oxidation.
tile.localization: Hypothalamic / brainstem appetite circuits (GLP-1R), pancreas, gut (GLP-1R), and liver (GCGR).
clinicalUses: Chronic weight management in Chinese adults with overweight or obesity (NMPA-approved June 2025)
Glycemic control in Chinese adults with type 2 diabetes (NMPA-approved September 2025)

dosing.quick: NMPA-approved Chinese labeling: once-weekly subcutaneous injection. Weight management dose 6 mg; 9 mg dose under supplemental NDA review for moderate-to-severe obesity. T2D dosing uses titration schedules studied in DREAMS trials. Not approved in the US or EU.

genes: GCG (proglucagon, parent of oxyntomodulin and GLP-1)

steps: Mazdutide binds GLP-1R (Ki ~28.6 nM human)
Gs activation → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP rise in pancreatic beta cells and hypothalamic/brainstem neurons
Glucose-dependent insulin secretion increases; postprandial glucagon is suppressed; appetite decreases; gastric emptying slows
Mazdutide binds GCGR (Ki ~17.7 nM human)
Gs/cAMP activation in hepatocytes → increased fatty acid oxidation and reduced hepatic lipogenesis
Increased thermogenesis and energy expenditure (implied from preclinical models); may improve liver fat content and insulin sensitivity

expanded.humanEvidence: Phase 3 GLORY-1 (Chinese adults with overweight/obesity, 4 and 6 mg doses for 48 weeks) showed superior weight loss vs placebo (~14% treatment difference at 6 mg). GLORY-2 (9 mg cohort) met primary endpoint with up to ~20% weight loss in moderate-to-severe obesity. DREAMS-1 and DREAMS-2 (published as Nature accelerated previews, 2025) established efficacy in treatment-naïve and oral-antidiabetic-refractory T2D respectively. DREAMS-3 demonstrated superiority of mazdutide vs semaglutide 1 mg on a composite HbA1c < 7% and ≥10% weight loss endpoint at week 32 in Chinese adults with T2D and obesity (48% vs 21%, p<0.0001).
expanded.animalEvidence: Preclinical mouse studies confirm mazdutide improves glucose control, reduces body weight, and increases energy expenditure; effects persist partially in Gcgr-knockout and Glp1r-knockout models, consistent with genuine dual agonism. Binds both human and murine GCGR / GLP-1R in the nanomolar range.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Mazdutide is a synthetic, long-acting analog of mammalian oxyntomodulin — an endogenous proglucagon-derived peptide that is itself a weak dual GLP-1R/GCGR agonist. Like semaglutide and liraglutide, mazdutide carries a fatty-acyl side chain enabling albumin binding and once-weekly subcutaneous dosing. Binding affinities reported for mazdutide TFA: human GCGR Ki 17.7 nM; human GLP-1R Ki 28.6 nM; mouse islet insulin secretion EC50 ~5.2 nM. Dual agonism is hypothesized to deliver the appetite-suppressing/insulinotropic benefits of GLP-1R agonism plus GCGR-mediated increases in energy expenditure and hepatic lipid mobilization, potentially mitigating the adaptive metabolic slowdown seen with GLP-1-only therapy. Evidence that balance matters: intentional bias in favor of GLP-1R limits glucagon-driven hyperglycemia.
expanded.safetyDetail: Across phase 1b through phase 3 trials, the adverse event profile mirrors the GLP-1 class — predominantly mild-to-moderate GI events (nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, decreased appetite) concentrated during dose titration. DREAMS-3 reported no severe hypoglycemia events; mild-to-moderate hypoglycemia incidence was comparable to dulaglutide 1.5 mg. No new safety signals distinct from the GLP-1 class have emerged in Chinese phase 3 data. Long-term cardiovascular and oncologic outcomes are not yet established in non-Chinese populations; EU/US regulatory approval has not been granted.

safetyRisks.label: Titration-phase nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Slowed gastric emptying is part of the intended mechanism
safetyRisks.harmSummary: GI events are the most common cause of discontinuation in the dual-agonist class; incidence is dose- and titration-dependent

safetyRisks.label: Hypoglycemia when combined with insulin / sulfonylurea

safetyRisks.harmSummary: Risk rises when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas

enhancementPotential.caveat: Not approved in the US or EU as of April 2026; off-label use in non-Chinese populations has no regulatory sanction and no registration trials outside China.

modifications: C18 fatty diacid acylation at a lysine side chain enabling albumin binding (>99% albumin-bound)
GLP-1-derived residues grafted onto glucagon scaffold for balanced dual receptor activation
identity.structureClass: glucagon-derived lipidated peptide (GCGR / GLP-1R dual agonist)

classification.mechanismFamily: GLP-1 receptor / glucagon receptor dual agonist (GLP-1R:GCGR activity bias ~8:1 favoring GLP-1R)

tile.mechanismSummary: 29-amino acid glucagon-derived lipidated peptide that dually agonizes the glucagon and GLP-1 receptors. GLP-1R activation reduces appetite, slows gastric emptying, and improves glucose-dependent insulin secretion; glucagon receptor agonism adds increased energy expenditure and direct hepatic lipid mobilization.
tile.localization: CNS appetite centers and gut (GLP-1R); liver (GCGR); pancreas.
clinicalUses: Investigational for MASH with fibrosis (LIVERAGE phase 3); FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation September 2024

dosing.quick: Investigational, not FDA-approved. Phase 3 trials use once-weekly subcutaneous injection with titration to target doses of 3.6 mg or 6.0 mg. Phase 2 obesity range tested: 2.4-4.8 mg weekly.

proteins: Human serum albumin (binding partner for the C18 diacid tail)

steps: Survodutide binds GLP-1R at very high potency (EC50 0.33 nM)
Gs activation → cAMP rise → glucose-dependent insulin secretion
Central effects: reduced appetite, delayed gastric emptying
Survodutide binds GCGR (EC50 0.52 nM)
Hepatocyte Gs/cAMP activation → increased fatty acid oxidation, ketogenesis, and decreased lipogenesis
Increased resting energy expenditure contributes to weight loss beyond GLP-1R alone; proposed to drive MASH fibrosis improvement

expanded.humanEvidence: Phase 1 PK established once-weekly dosing feasibility with ~6-day half-life (>99% albumin-bound). Phase 2 obesity (NCT04667377) showed up to ~19% mean body weight reduction at 46 weeks at the 4.8 mg dose. Phase 2 T2D on metformin background showed dose-dependent HbA1c reductions at 16 weeks. Phase 2 MASH (NCT04771273, Sanyal et al., NEJM 2024) randomized 293 adults with biopsy-confirmed MASH and F1-F3 fibrosis to 2.4, 4.8, or 6.0 mg or placebo for 48 weeks — primary endpoint (MASH improvement without worsening fibrosis) met in 47/62/43% vs 14% placebo; fibrosis improvement in 34/36/34% vs 22%. Phase 3 SYNCHRONIZE-1/-2 (obesity), SYNCHRONIZE-CVOT (CV outcomes in ~4,935 participants), LIVERAGE/LIVERAGE-Cirrhosis (MASH), and SYNCHRONIZE-JP/China obesity trials are ongoing.
expanded.animalEvidence: Preclinical DIO models (Zimmermann et al., Mol Metab 2022) established superior anti-obesity efficacy relative to selective GLP-1R agonism; cryo-EM structures of survodutide bound to GLP-1R and GCGR (with G protein) support a dual agonist binding mode resembling oxyntomodulin.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Unlike mazdutide (oxyntomodulin analog), survodutide is a glucagon analog reshaped with GLP-1-like residues to produce a balanced dual agonist biased modestly toward GLP-1R activity (activity ratio ~1:8 GCGR:GLP-1R). It is 29 amino acids with a C18 fatty diacid enabling albumin binding (>99%) and a ~6-day plasma half-life. cAMP EC50 values (CHO-K1): GCGR 0.52 nM, GLP-1R 0.33 nM. Compared with native peptides, survodutide is ~4× less potent than GLP-1 at GLP-1R and ~22× less potent than glucagon at GCGR — resembling the dual agonist oxyntomodulin. Dual engagement is hypothesized to couple the appetite/insulin effects of GLP-1R to direct hepatic effects of GCGR (increased fatty acid oxidation, reduced steatosis) that may underpin the MASH fibrosis benefit.
expanded.safetyDetail: Across phase 1-3 trials, GI adverse events (nausea, diarrhea, vomiting) are the most common events and are concentrated during dose escalation. In the phase 2 MASH trial, survodutide adverse event rates were: nausea 66% vs 23% placebo, diarrhea 49% vs 23%, vomiting 41% vs 4%; serious adverse events 8% vs 7%. No unexpected hepatic or cardiovascular signal has been reported in completed trials, and a phase 1 trial in cirrhosis showed no need for dose adjustment in hepatic impairment. Long-term cardiovascular safety awaits SYNCHRONIZE-CVOT. Not approved anywhere.

safetyRisks.label: Nausea / vomiting / diarrhea during titration
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Consistent with the GLP-1 class; mostly mild-to-moderate and dose-dependent
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Higher incidence than GLP-1-only agonists in some comparisons; can drive discontinuation during rapid escalation

safetyRisks.helpSummary: GLP-1 bias in molecule design mitigates this risk; net effect in trials is glucose-lowering

enhancementPotential.caveat: Investigational; no regulatory approval anywhere as of April 2026. Off-label research or self-experimentation is not supported by any approved label.

Binds GLP-1R at a site distinct from the endogenous peptide's orthosteric pocket (cryo-EM characterized)
G-protein-biased partial agonist: activates Gs/cAMP with minimal β-arrestin recruitment
identity.structureClass: oral small-molecule (non-peptide) GLP-1 receptor partial agonist

classification.peptideClass: non-peptide small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist

tile.mechanismSummary: Oral, once-daily small-molecule partial agonist of the GLP-1 receptor that biases signaling toward Gs/cAMP over β-arrestin recruitment, mimicking the appetite-suppressing, gastric-emptying, and glucose-dependent insulinotropic effects of peptide GLP-1 analogs without requiring injection.
tile.localization: GLP-1R expressing tissues — pancreatic beta cells, gut, and CNS appetite centers.
clinicalUses: Chronic weight management in adults with obesity, or overweight with at least one weight-related comorbidity (FDA-approved as Foundayo, April 1, 2026)
Investigational for type 2 diabetes (regulatory submission planned 2026)
Under study for obstructive sleep apnea, hypertension, and osteoarthritis knee pain
sideEffects: Boxed warning: risk of thyroid C-cell tumors

dosing.quick: FDA-approved oral tablet (Foundayo), once daily, any time of day, with or without food. Start 0.8 mg daily; increase to 2.5 mg after ≥30 days, then 5.5 mg after ≥30 days, with further escalation to 9, 14.5, or 17.2 mg at ≥30-day intervals based on response and tolerability.

proteins: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor (GLP-1R, class B GPCR)

steps: Orforglipron binds GLP-1R at a site that partially overlaps the peptide orthosteric pocket (cryo-EM, Kawai et al. PNAS 2020)
Gs coupling → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP rise in pancreatic beta cells and neurons
Minimal β-arrestin recruitment — hypothesized to reduce receptor desensitization
Downstream: glucose-dependent insulin secretion increases, appetite decreases, gastric emptying slows

expanded.humanEvidence: FDA-approved April 1, 2026 for chronic weight management based on the ATTAIN phase 3 program (>4,500 participants). ATTAIN-1 (NCT05869903, n=3,127, 72 weeks): mean weight change −7.5%/−8.4%/−11.2% with 6/12/36 mg vs −2.1% placebo (efficacy estimand, highest dose delivered ~27.3 lbs / 12.4% mean loss); ≥10% weight loss in 54.6% of 36 mg group vs 12.9% placebo. ATTAIN-2 (NCT in obesity + T2D, 72 weeks): up to 9.6% mean weight loss at 36 mg. ACHIEVE-1 (NCT, 40 weeks, n=559 early T2D): HbA1c reductions −1.24/−1.47/−1.48 percentage points at 3/12/36 mg vs −0.41 placebo; weight loss up to 7.6% at 36 mg. ATTAIN-MAINTAIN demonstrated weight maintenance after switching from injectable semaglutide or tirzepatide. Adverse event profile is consistent with the GLP-1 class (GI-predominant, mild-to-moderate).
expanded.animalEvidence: Discovered by Chugai Pharmaceutical (OWL833) and licensed to Lilly (2018). Preclinical characterization (Kawai et al., PNAS 2020) established cryo-EM structure of LY3502970 bound to GLP-1R, documenting biased partial agonism with cAMP induction and near-absent β-arrestin recruitment; high GLP-1R selectivity over other class B GPCRs; pharmacokinetics suitable for oral dosing.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Orforglipron is structurally a small organic molecule (MW 882.96; C48H48F2N10O5) — not a peptide — that occupies a pocket adjacent to the endogenous GLP-1 binding site on GLP-1R. It is a partial, G-protein-biased agonist: it raises cAMP comparably to peptide GLP-1 analogs but recruits little β-arrestin, which has been hypothesized to reduce receptor desensitization and possibly improve tolerability. Downstream effects mirror peptide GLP-1R agonists — glucose-dependent insulin secretion, glucagon suppression, delayed gastric emptying, and reduced appetite. Because it is a small molecule, oral bioavailability does not require permeation-enhancing formulation, so unlike oral semaglutide (Rybelsus), Foundayo has no food- or water-intake timing restrictions.
expanded.safetyDetail: FDA label carries a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors, inherited from the GLP-1 class precedent; contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2. Additional warnings: acute pancreatitis, severe GI adverse reactions, acute kidney injury due to volume depletion, hypoglycemia (especially with insulin or sulfonylureas), serious hypersensitivity reactions, diabetic retinopathy complications in T2D, acute gallbladder disease, and pulmonary aspiration risk during general anesthesia/deep sedation. In ATTAIN-1, treatment discontinuation due to adverse events occurred in 5.3-10.3% of orforglipron patients vs 2.7% placebo; no hepatic safety signal was observed.

safetyRisks.label: Thyroid C-cell tumors (boxed warning)
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Risk inferred from rodent data seen with peptide GLP-1 class; no confirmed human signal to date
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Contraindicated in personal/family history of MTC or MEN 2; counsel patients to report neck masses, hoarseness, dysphagia, dyspnea

safetyRisks.label: Nausea / vomiting / constipation / pancreatitis risk
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Mild-to-moderate and largely titration-linked; less nausea than some peptide GLP-1 analogs in some comparisons
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Nausea 28.9-35.9% across doses vs 10.4% placebo in ATTAIN-1; acute pancreatitis and gallbladder disease are labeled risks

enhancementPotential.summary: Off-label use in healthy, lean, non-obese adults is not supported by any approved indication. Weight loss magnitude (~12% at highest dose) is meaningful but modest compared with injectable GLP-1 or GLP-1/GIP agonists.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Only indicated for adults with obesity or overweight with a weight-related comorbidity. Not approved for T2D as of April 2026. Thyroid C-cell tumor boxed warning applies regardless of indication.

claims.value: ATTAIN-1 (n=3,127, 72 weeks): mean body weight change −7.5%/−8.4%/−11.2% at 6/12/36 mg vs −2.1% placebo (efficacy estimand); ≥10% weight loss in 54.6% of the 36 mg arm vs 12.9% placebo. ACHIEVE-1 (early T2D, 40 weeks): HbA1c reductions −1.24 to −1.48 percentage points vs −0.41 placebo
claims.population: ATTAIN-1: adults with obesity without diabetes; ACHIEVE-1: adults with early T2D

claims.value: Non-peptide small molecule (MW 882.96, C48H48F2N10O5); G-protein-biased partial agonist of GLP-1R with minimal β-arrestin recruitment; cryo-EM-characterized binding at a site adjacent to the peptide orthosteric pocket
Boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors; contraindicated in personal/family history of MTC or MEN 2; AE discontinuation 5.3-10.3% (orforglipron) vs 2.7% (placebo) in ATTAIN-1

citations.title: Orforglipron — Drug Hunter molecule profile
citations.notes: Provides structural and discovery context (Chugai OWL833 → Lilly LY3502970).

modifications: 37-amino acid amylin analog built on a pramlintide-like backbone (not human amylin) to reduce fibrillation
Reported position substitutions include 14E and 17R forming a stabilizing salt bridge and a C-terminal proline / tyrosine modification (per Novo Nordisk medicinal chemistry paper)
N-terminal (Lys1) acylation with a C20 eicosanedioic fatty diacid via a γ-glutamic acid linker, enabling reversible albumin binding
C-terminal amidation; intramolecular Cys-Cys disulfide consistent with amylin scaffold
identity.structureClass: long-acting acylated amylin analog (DACRA-like: dual amylin and calcitonin receptor agonist pharmacology)

classification.mechanismFamily: non-selective amylin receptor (AMY1/AMY2/AMY3) and calcitonin receptor (CTR) agonist

tile.mechanismSummary: Long-acting, lipidated amylin analog that non-selectively agonizes AMY1, AMY2, AMY3, and the calcitonin receptor. Acts on area postrema and hypothalamic circuits to suppress appetite, slows gastric emptying, and inhibits postprandial glucagon — engineered for once-weekly dosing via albumin binding.
tile.localization: Area postrema / brainstem satiety circuits; hypothalamic appetite centers; peripheral amylin-receptor sites on stomach and pancreatic alpha cells.
clinicalUses: Investigational for chronic weight management, primarily as part of the fixed-dose CagriSema combination with semaglutide 2.4 mg
CagriSema NDA submitted to the FDA (December 2025); not yet approved as of April 21, 2026

dosing.quick: Investigational; not FDA-approved. Phase 3 use (CagriSema): cagrilintide 2.4 mg once weekly subcutaneous, co-administered with semaglutide 2.4 mg. Monotherapy phase 2 titration reached 4.5 mg weekly.

proteins: Human serum albumin (binding partner for the C20 diacid tail)
receptors: Calcitonin receptor (CTR, unopposed) — cagrilintide is a non-selective DACRA

steps: Cagrilintide binds AMY1/AMY3/CTR in the area postrema and nucleus tractus solitarius
Gs / adenylyl cyclase / cAMP activation in satiety neurons
Projection to hypothalamic appetite circuits reduces meal size and caloric intake
Single-dose preclinical food-intake suppression lasts 48-60 h vs ~6 h for pramlintide
Cagrilintide engages peripheral and central amylin receptors
Postprandial glucagon secretion is suppressed; gastric emptying is slowed
Contributes to blood-glucose control in T2D combinations
Amylin receptor pathway is non-overlapping with GLP-1R signaling
Combining cagrilintide 2.4 mg with semaglutide 2.4 mg yields additive weight loss in REDEFINE 1 (~22.7% vs 16.1% semaglutide mono vs 11.8% cagrilintide mono at 68 weeks)

expanded.humanEvidence: Phase 1b study in adults with overweight/obesity established safety and PK (half-life 159-195 h; Cmax at 24-72 h post-dose) for once-weekly dosing. Phase 2 dose-finding (up to 4.5 mg weekly) produced ~10.8% mean weight loss over 26 weeks as monotherapy, exceeding liraglutide 3.0 mg. In the phase 3 REDEFINE 1 trial (NCT05669755; n=3,417; 68 weeks), cagrilintide 2.4 mg monotherapy produced ~11.8% weight loss, semaglutide 2.4 mg ~16.1%, and the CagriSema combination ~22.7%, all relative to ~2.3% with placebo; 91.9% of CagriSema participants achieved ≥5% weight reduction vs 31.5% placebo. REDEFINE 2 in adults with T2D showed 13.7% weight loss with CagriSema. Novo Nordisk submitted a CagriSema NDA to the FDA in December 2025 for weight management.
expanded.animalEvidence: Development work (Kruse et al., Nitsche et al., and Novo Nordisk medicinal chemistry program, J Med Chem 2021) established the 37-aa pramlintide-like backbone, N-terminal C20 fatty diacid attachment via γ-Glu, and structural mutations for helix stabilization and fibril resistance. Cryo-EM structures (Nature Communications 2025) of cagrilintide bound to AMY1, AMY2, AMY3, and CTR reveal a non-selective binding mode distinct from both rat amylin and salmon calcitonin. Preclinical food-intake assays show sustained anorectic effects of 48-60 h per single dose.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Cagrilintide is a 37-amino acid peptide modeled on the pramlintide backbone (chosen over human amylin to blunt amyloidogenicity), with additional engineered substitutions believed to form a stabilizing intramolecular salt bridge (14E/17R reported) and a fatty-acid attachment point at the N-terminal lysine. Attachment of a C20 eicosanedioic fatty diacid via a γ-glutamic acid linker produces high-affinity, reversible albumin binding and an elimination half-life of 159-195 hours — a ~200-fold extension versus pramlintide's ~48-minute half-life — enabling once-weekly subcutaneous dosing. Pharmacologically, cagrilintide is a DACRA (dual amylin and calcitonin receptor agonist): unlike pramlintide (selective for AMYRs), cagrilintide retains meaningful activity at the bare calcitonin receptor. This may contribute both to efficacy and to safety monitoring (historical concern about calcitonin-containing products and malignancy, though no such signal has been observed with cagrilintide).
expanded.safetyDetail: Phase 1-3 data show a safety profile dominated by transient, titration-related GI adverse events (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea). REDEFINE 1 reported single-digit discontinuation rates. No new class-defining safety signal has been reported as of April 2026. As an amylin/calcitonin receptor family agonist, long-term monitoring for C-cell effects is theoretically prudent but not observed clinically to date. The medicinal-chemistry literature noted historical concern about calcitonin products and malignancy but did not identify such a signal with cagrilintide. Cardiovascular outcomes data for the combination (CagriSema) versus GLP-1-only comparators are not yet mature.

safetyRisks.label: Titration-phase nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Most events are mild-to-moderate and concentrated during dose escalation
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Discontinuation rates in REDEFINE 1 were low single-digit, but GI events are the dominant AE category

safetyRisks.label: Calcitonin-receptor family activation — theoretical C-cell concern
safetyRisks.helpSummary: No increased malignancy signal observed in trials to date
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Historical pharmacologic precedent prompted safety screening; cagrilintide's pharmacologic resemblance to pramlintide (no malignancy signal) is reassuring but not definitive

enhancementPotential.summary: No data in healthy, lean adults. All phase 3 efficacy is in adults with overweight/obesity (±T2D). As of April 21, 2026, cagrilintide and CagriSema are investigational; the CagriSema NDA filed in December 2025 has not been acted on.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Off-label use outside trials is not supported by regulatory approval; the 'research-use-only' material widely sold is not a medicine. Long-term safety data are limited.

missingEvidence: Long-term cardiovascular outcomes data for CagriSema vs tirzepatide
Direct head-to-head weight-composition (fat vs lean mass) comparisons with GLP-1 or GLP-1/GIP combinations
anecdotalUse: Widely sold in the 'research peptide' gray market as a cagrilintide/semaglutide stack; this use is not supported by any approved label and the products are not quality-controlled for human use

claims.value: Investigational worldwide. CagriSema (cagrilintide 2.4 mg + semaglutide 2.4 mg) NDA submitted to the FDA on December 18, 2025 for chronic weight management; no approval decision as of April 21, 2026. Cagrilintide monotherapy remains in development (RENEW).
claims.population: adults with obesity or overweight with comorbidity

claims.value: REDEFINE 1 (phase 3, n=3,417, 68 weeks): CagriSema ~22.7% mean weight loss vs 16.1% semaglutide, 11.8% cagrilintide mono, and placebo; 91.9% of CagriSema arm achieved ≥5% weight loss vs 31.5% placebo. REDEFINE 2 (T2D): ~13.7% weight loss with CagriSema.
claims.population: adults with obesity or overweight with comorbidity (REDEFINE 1); adults with T2D (REDEFINE 2)

claims.value: Half-life 159-195 h (6.6-8.1 days); median Cmax 24-72 h; exposure is dose-proportional over 0.16-4.5 mg; once-weekly subcutaneous dosing supported by albumin binding via the C20 fatty diacid

Non-selective agonist of AMY1, AMY2, AMY3, and the bare calcitonin receptor (DACRA-type pharmacology); based on a pramlintide-like 37-aa backbone with N-terminal C20 fatty diacid via a γ-Glu linker for albumin binding
claims.population: recombinant receptor assays (HEK293, CHO); cryo-EM with each receptor
